name: Oceanus
description: Oceanus, in Greek mythology, is the Titan god of the great, all-encircling river or stream that was believed to surround the world. He is often depicted as an elderly, bearded man with crab claws for horns and a serpentine lower body, symbolizing the vastness and primordial nature of the ocean. Unlike Poseidon, who ruled the seas, Oceanus represented the freshwater source from which all rivers, springs, and seas originated. Oceanus maintained a relatively passive role in the Olympian pantheon, largely remaining neutral in the conflicts between the Titans and the Olympians. He is considered a benevolent figure, a source of life and nourishment for the world. His domain extended to the boundaries of the known world, a liminal space between the mortal realm and the unknown. He is often invoked as a witness to oaths and agreements, underscoring his foundational role in the cosmic order. His importance diminished with the rise of Poseidon and the growing understanding of geography, but he remained a powerful symbol of the earth's primordial waters.
subtitle: The Titan of the World-Ocean

keyMyths.title: The Birth of the Rivers
keyMyths.description: Oceanus is credited as the father of all rivers and springs. He and his consort Tethys birthed thousands of children, each embodying a specific waterway. These rivers not only provided sustenance for the land but also served as pathways for trade and communication, highlighting Oceanus' vital role in the ancient world. The sheer number of his offspring illustrates the vast and interconnected nature of the earth's water systems, all emanating from Oceanus himself.

keyMyths.title: Oceanus and the Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: During the Titanomachy, the great war between the Titans and the Olympian gods, Oceanus chose not to side with his fellow Titans. He remained neutral, recognizing the inevitable victory of the Olympians. This decision allowed him and his descendants to retain their positions of power and influence after the war. His neutrality highlights his wisdom and foresight, recognizing the changing tides of power in the cosmos.

keyMyths.title: The Journey of the Sun
keyMyths.description: In some accounts, the sun-god Helios was believed to travel around Oceanus each night in a golden cup, returning to the east to begin his journey anew. This myth emphasizes Oceanus' role as the boundary of the world and the pathway for celestial bodies. It illustrates the cyclical nature of time and the constant movement of the cosmos, all within the embrace of the world-ocean.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A foundational text detailing the genealogy of the gods and the creation of the cosmos, including Oceanus' role.

sources.author: Apollodorus
